---
date: "2026-06-22"
---

## The Result in One Plain Sentence

- Part 2 — stating the construction in one plain sentence

::: notes
Welcome back to Week Four-point-five, part two of twelve. In part one we framed the big idea: when you cannot find an instrument, you can build one from a national part and a local part. Now we state the whole construction in one plain sentence, meet the two words at its heart, and write down the two ingredients it assembles.
:::

## The Result in One Plain Sentence

- Take each industry's **national** growth rate — no town caused it
- Reweight by **how much each town depended** on that industry
- That predicted shock is your instrument
- **Shifts** = national industry growth rates
- **Shares** = local exposure weights at the start

::: notes
Here is the result in one plain sentence. To get an instrument for how hard a local economy was hit, take the national growth rate of each industry — a shock that no single town can have caused — and reweight those national shocks by how much each town depended on each industry to begin with. A town heavily invested in industries that boomed nationally gets a high predicted shock; a town tied to nationally-declining industries gets a low one. That predicted shock is your instrument. The whole idea lives in two words. Shifts are the national industry growth rates — how fast nationwide employment in autos, finance, or retail grew this year. Shares are the local exposure weights — what fraction of a region's jobs were in each industry at the start, before this year's shocks landed.
:::

## Why the Lever Is Clean

- A region's realized fortunes mix **two** things
- The **national tide** lifting its industries — exogenous to the region
- Purely **local** stuff — scandals, booms, the confounders we fear
- Shift-share keeps **only** the national ingredient
- The realized local shock is the contaminated treatment it instruments for

::: notes
Why would this be a good instrument for a region's actual economic shock? Because a region's realized fortunes mix two things. First, the national tide lifting or sinking its industries — Toledo did not cause the national auto cycle, so that piece is exogenous to the region. Second, purely local stuff — a local scandal, a local boom, the very confounders we fear. The shift-share construction keeps only the first ingredient. It predicts what should have happened to a region if it had simply ridden the national industry waves at its initial exposure, throwing away everything local. That predicted-from-national-tides shock is the clean lever; the realized local shock is the contaminated treatment it instruments for. Everything technical from here is an elaboration of that sentence, plus the long-delayed answer to why the lever is clean — which you can defend from the share side or the shift side, and those two defenses are the two halves of this lesson.
:::

## Key Takeaway

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
In one sentence: take **national** industry growth rates (the **shifts**), reweight them by a region's **baseline exposure** (the **shares**), and the share-weighted sum is your instrument. It works because it keeps **only** the national tide — exogenous to the region — and throws away the local confounders. Next: build the object with real numbers.
:::

::: notes
To summarize part two: in one sentence, you take national industry growth rates — the shifts — reweight them by a region's baseline industry exposure — the shares — and the share-weighted sum is your instrument. It is a good instrument because it keeps only the national tide, which is exogenous to any single region, and throws away the purely local stuff where the confounders live. The realized local shock that we actually want to study is contaminated by those confounders; the predicted shock built from national tides is clean. In part three we stop talking in words and build the object concretely, because the formula is opaque but the arithmetic is transparent.
:::
